steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The student wants to present cultural analysis to an audience unfamiliar with the concept. An unfamiliar audience needs to know what cultural analysis is — a definition — not an example of it in action or a comparison to a different method.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'An approach to art criticism, cultural analysis considers the cultural trends, beliefs, and values of an artwork’s time.': Directly defines cultural analysis for someone who has never heard of it. ✓
  • Choice B — 'An emphasis analysis of Still Life with Lobster might consider how the painting’s sharply contrasting colors place primary emphasis on the lobster in the foreground.': Describes emphasis analysis, not cultural analysis. An unfamiliar audience learns nothing about cultural analysis here. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Les Demoiselles d’Avignon’s portrayal of fragmented human figures challenged conventions of beauty during a time of cultural upheaval (early twentieth century).': Gives an example of what a cultural analysis might notice, but never names or defines cultural analysis. An unfamiliar reader has no idea this is about cultural analysis at all. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Emphasis analysis differs from cultural analysis in that emphasis analysis examines how visual elements are weighted in an artwork’s composition.': Defines emphasis analysis and only mentions cultural analysis by contrast. It tells the audience nothing about what cultural analysis actually is. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
